title: Stottie cake
subtitle: The dense, flat bread roll of northeast England
excerpt: The stottie is the bread of the northeast: a thick, flat disc, heavy in the hand, with a tight crumb and a very slight chew. It comes from the practice of using up leftover dough; flattened and baked on the bottom of the oven. The result is a roll that holds a filling without becoming soggy. Pease pudding and cooked ham are the traditional fillings, but any sturdy filling works.
sourceNotes: The stottie is specifically associated with Tyneside and County Durham in northeast England. The name may derive from the Geordie verb 'stot' meaning to bounce or be resilient. Recipes appear in regional food guides and in Bob Arora's 'Traditional Northeast Recipes'; Florence White's 'Good Things in England' (1932) mentions Northumbrian bread styles. The stottie traditionally uses baker's leftover dough baked in the cooled oven.

recipe.yieldDescription: 2 large stotties (25 cm each), enough for 4 filled rolls
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze whole or sliced. Thaw at room temperature or refresh for 5 minutes at 180 degrees C fan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be mixed and left for a slow bulk ferment of 4 to 6 hours at room temperature rather than 75 minutes.

content.text: The stottie's dense crumb comes from a tighter-than-usual dough (63% hydration) and a shorter proof. It bakes on the bottom of the oven so the base cooks first, producing the characteristic slightly hard base that gives the roll its carrying capacity. Lard in the dough is traditional and adds a slight richness; vegetable fat can be substituted without much difference to the crumb.

Combine 500 g flour, 7 g yeast, and 10 g salt. Rub in 20 g lard until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add 315 ml tepid water and mix to a firm dough. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth. The dough will be noticeably stiffer than a standard loaf dough; this is correct.
Cover and leave at room temperature until doubled, about 60 minutes.

Knock back and divide into two pieces. Shape each into a ball, then press firmly with the palm of your hand into a disc about 25 cm across and 2 cm thick. Transfer to lightly floured baking trays. Use two fingers or a fork handle to press a hole through the centre of each, almost but not quite through to the tray. This traditional dimple prevents the stottie from puffing up unevenly in the oven. Leave for 15 minutes without a cover; the surface should form a light skin.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Place the trays on the bottom shelf. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the stotties are pale gold on top and the bases are firm and slightly darkened. The colour should be muted rather than deep; a heavily browned stottie is overbaked. Cool flat on a rack. Slice through the equator to fill.

items.problem: Stottie puffed up in the oven into a dome
items.solution: The centre dimple was not deep enough or the dough was under-proved. Press the dimple all the way through, leaving only a thin base of dough, and make sure the second rest completes fully before baking.

items.problem: Crumb is too open and airy
items.solution: The dough was too wet or kneaded too long. The stottie crumb is deliberately tight and dense. Use the exact water quantity and resist the urge to add more flour — the dough is firm, not crumbly.

items.problem: Top is pale and soft, base is very hard
items.solution: The oven temperature was too high or the tray was too close to the bottom element. The stottie bakes from the base but should not have a burnt underside. Move to the shelf above the bottom, or reduce to 190 degrees C.
